summary: Gruber pushes back against the theory that Apple keeps Lightning on iPhones primarily as a money grab through MFi licensing revenue. He argues that MFi revenue is trivial by Apple's standards and that the real calculus involves carefully weighing pros and cons for each product. He points out that hundreds of millions of iPhone users already own Lightning accessories and would be unhappy about a forced switch, noting that Lightning-to-USB-C is more of a sidestep than a clear upgrade. He cites Apple's adoption of Qi wireless charging, USB-C on iPads and MacBooks, and varied headphone jack decisions as evidence that Apple makes pragmatic per-product trade-offs rather than following a single greedy motive.
key_points: MFi licensing revenue from Lightning accessories is negligible by Apple's standards and not a plausible primary motivation for keeping the port
Switching iPhone from Lightning to USB-C is a sidestep rather than a clear upgrade, unlike the 30-pin to Lightning transition which was unambiguously better
Hundreds of millions of iPhone users have Lightning cables embedded throughout their lives and would strongly resist a forced switch
Apple's adoption of Qi charging, USB-C on iPads and MacBooks, and selective headphone jack removal all demonstrate pragmatic per-product decision-making rather than a unified money-grab strategy
Reducing complex multi-factor product decisions to a single explanation like 'control' or 'greed' is facile
money_quotes: It's loose-change-under-the-couch-cushion revenue by Apple's astonishingly high standards.
The pro-USB-C crowd, to me, often comes across as ideological.
In 15 generations of iPhones, Apple has changed the connector once. And that one time was a clear win in every single regard.
Changing from Lightning to USB-C is not so clearly an upgrade at all. It's a sidestep.
They have Lightning chargers in their kitchens, cars, purses, backpacks, and bedrooms. All things considered, they do not want to replace any of them, let alone all of them.
"Apple's just trying to force everyone to buy $160 AirPods" is not a nuanced argument.
To say that there's a reason that the iPhones 13 still use Lightning, any singular reason, is facile.
key_insight: Apple's decision to keep Lightning on iPhones reflects pragmatic product trade-offs weighed across hundreds of millions of users, not a simplistic revenue grab from MFi licensing.
